_comment: Aperio import fixture — a FORWARD-LOOKING PLAN / work-in-progress for Mitigram interview prep and a DOCUMENT-DRIVEN BGAPI TradeFinance take-home build. The spine: Mitigram's product is AI document interpretation for trade finance (OCR + ML turning unstructured/SWIFT trade docs into structured data); Aperio's docgraph + vision path is the already-built sibling of that; the capstone ingests a real trade document and produces a FinancingRequest. Planned entries are written as intent ('we'll', 'we plan', 'we assume') and tagged 'wip'/'planned'; a pinned 'status' memory states this so any hooked model knows it is in progress, not done. Genuinely-real history (the SQL win, Aperio + docgraph + vision existing, Octopus, domain/reference facts) stays factual. The honesty doctrine is explicit: we built the document-ingestion engine + a vision PoC, NOT Mitigram's production OCR/ML — never claim otherwise. Rewrite a planned entry to past tense only once that step is actually finished. Every entry is tagged 'mitigram-prep'; Aperio self-knowledge entries also carry 'aperio-identity'. Import via MCP import_data then backfill_embeddings. Designed for reliable recall by local Ollama models (see dev-side.md).
_how_to_import: MCP: import_data { input_path: '/Users/lk/Projects/BaiGanio/aperio/me/mitigram-memories.aperio.json' } then backfill_embeddings. Demo recall: recall with tag 'mitigram-prep' (or 'aperio-identity' for the self-knowledge set). Pinned 'status' memory flags the whole set as work-in-progress.

memories.title: STATUS — this is a PLAN, work in progress (not done yet)
memories.content: Notice for any AI model reading the 'mitigram-prep' memory set: this is a PLAN for interview preparation and a DOCUMENT-DRIVEN take-home-style build we INTEND to do over ~10 days. It is NOT completed work. Read every 'wip'/'planned' entry as intent — 'we'll do X', 'we plan Y', 'we assume Z' — not as accomplishment. The document-driven TradeFinance slice in BGAPI (ingest a trade document -> extract fields -> FinancingRequest -> approve -> read) does not exist yet; we are about to build it. Some entries are genuinely real already (the >30s-to-<3s SQL win; that Aperio exists with codegraph, docgraph and a vision path; the Octopus/4hundred work; domain/reference facts; the Aperio self-knowledge entries) — those stay factual. Only rewrite a planned entry into past tense once that specific step is actually finished and verified. Until then, never present the planned items as accomplished.

memories.title: Mitigram interview — role and target stack
memories.content: Preparing for Senior Fullstack Developer (AI-Enhanced) at Mitigram, a Swedish fintech digital trade-finance platform. Fully remote, senior, EUR 2,500-6,000 net/mo. Their PRODUCT is AI document interpretation for trade finance (see the dedicated 'product DNA' memory). Target stack: .NET 8/C# 13, DDD, CQRS via Mediator, SQL Server + Dapper, Redis, xUnit/TDD, Svelte 4/Angular/TypeScript, Vite/Vitest, Azure, Docker + Kubernetes, JWT/OAuth/SAML, AI-first (Claude Code).

memories.title: Mitigram product DNA — AI document interpretation for trade finance
memories.content: Mitigram's differentiator (per mitigram.com/our-technology): AI-powered OCR combined with machine-learning algorithms that interpret trade documents and turn them into structured, actionable trade-finance data. It handles BOTH structured inputs (SWIFT MT messages) and unstructured ones (PDFs, scans, emails), minimizes manual data entry, and can learn to POPULATE MISSING DATA automatically. Instruments: Letters of Credit (import/export), bank guarantees and standby LCs, receivables/invoice financing, LC price confirmations. Workflow: RFQ -> compare quotes across banks -> digital approval. Multi-channel: web, email, SWIFT, EBICS. PowerBI embedded for analytics. Compliance: ISO 27001, DORA, full auditability. The document-to-structured-data step is the hard, valuable core — and it is exactly the problem class I have already built around (see the docgraph memory).

memories.title: Mitigram interview process — two rounds
memories.content: Round 1 is a ~30-min non-technical intro/fit call (recruiter or hiring manager): rapport, motivation, logistics, salary (state a senior-anchored range within EUR 2,500-6,000 net/mo), time-zone overlap (Bulgaria EET vs Sweden CET, ~1h). Round 2+ is the technical deep-dive with the dev team — open it on their product (document interpretation) and the docgraph parallel if you can. Keep tech high-level in round 1; go deep in round 2.

memories.title: BGAPI is the interview practice codebase
memories.content: Our planned practice vehicle is my own .NET 10 platform BGAPI (TeamKepler ecosystem). It already exercises ~70% of Mitigram's stack: C# 13, EF Core, SQL Server, Redis, JWT (IdentityServer4 to Keycloak), Docker, xUnit/Moq, Azure. The gaps it lacks are exactly what Mitigram screens for: DDD, CQRS/Mediator, Dapper, TDD discipline, Svelte — plus the document-intake/extraction flow that mirrors their product. The plan is to close each gap by building one document-driven feature on BGAPI. Not started yet.

memories.title: Document-driven TradeFinance slice — the planned take-home artifact
memories.content: We plan to build one additive bounded context in BGAPI that mirrors Mitigram's flow: a document goes in, structured data and a financing decision come out. POST /api/financing-requests/intake (multipart: a trade doc — invoice/LC as PDF/DOCX, or an image for the vision PoC) -> an extraction step yields fields -> CreateFinancingRequestCommand carries them -> FinancingRequest aggregate (Draft->Submitted) -> ApproveFinancingRequestCommand (credit-limit invariant) -> GetFinancingRequestQuery (Dapper read). Built test-first, with a Svelte+Vitest UI that renders the EXTRACTED fields, deployed to a k3s homelab. The intent: one slice that demonstrates document ingestion/extraction PLUS DDD, CQRS, Dapper, TDD, Svelte and Kubernetes on a real codebase. Status: not started.

memories.title: We'll put extraction behind ITradeDocumentExtractor with a gap-to-confirm rule
memories.content: Planned design for the document layer (the part that mirrors Mitigram's product): an ITradeDocumentExtractor interface returns the extracted fields PLUS a list of gaps (fields the document didn't yield). Two impls: (a) a deterministic text/DOCX parser for structured invoices/LCs, (b) a vision PoC impl for scans (see the vision memory). A field the document lacks becomes a 'gap to confirm', NOT a crash — mirroring Mitigram's 'populate missing data' behavior, where a human confirms rather than re-types. The domain (FinancingRequest) never knows the source of the fields; it just receives structured data plus a DocumentReference back to the source doc (traceability/audit). The seam is the senior signal: production OCR/ML drops into the same interface.

memories.title: We'll do the vision PoC via Aperio's vision tools + a configurable local VLM
memories.content: Vision step (the OCR analog): hand a scanned-document image to Aperio's vision tools (read_image / preprocess_image / describe_image, backed by lib/helpers/imageBridge.js) to pull amount, currency, counterparty, IBAN, invoice number. The local VLM is DECIDED: qwen3-vl:8b (set OLLAMA_VLM_MODEL=qwen3-vl:8b; Aperio's code default is qwen2.5vl:7b — override it). Why qwen3-vl:8b: it leads OCRBench (~896-910, beating InternVL 2.5-78B at 9x fewer params), so the OCR is genuinely strong not a toy; ~11 GB Q4_K_M at 8k ctx (vs qwen2.5-vl:7b ~15 GB) so it fits the 16 GB machine; 256K native context; runs fully local (data residency). Honest boundary: I INTEGRATED this off-the-shelf VLM as the OCR step — I did not train it, and I did not build Mitigram's trained ML field-classification pipeline — see the honest-bridge memory.

memories.title: Honest OCR/ML bridge — never claim we built Mitigram's model
memories.content: The honesty doctrine for document AI: I BUILT the document-ingestion engine (docgraph: ingest -> normalize -> structure -> chunk -> hybrid search -> reference extraction), and I INTEGRATED a best-in-class open VLM (qwen3-vl:8b, which leads OCRBench) as the pixels->fields OCR step — so the OCR is real and strong, not a toy. What I did NOT do: train the VLM, or build Mitigram's trained ML field-classification pipeline (the learned 'populate missing data', discrepancy logic at production scale) — that is their IP and the genuinely hard part. The senior framing turns this into a bridge: 'I built the whole arc around the model and my OCR step runs a top OCRBench VLM locally, so I know exactly where your trained ML slots in — in my capstone the extractor sits behind an interface precisely so your production ML drops into the same seam.' Never say 'I built OCR', 'I trained a VLM', or 'I built an ML pipeline'. Always say 'I built the engine and integrated a strong open VLM as the OCR step, and I designed the seam your trained ML lives in.'

memories.title: ACTION — source 2-3 sample trade documents into me/trade-docs/ EARLY
memories.content: Do this early, before the call: get 2-3 real sample trade documents — a UCP-600 specimen Letter of Credit (PDF), a commercial-invoice template (DOCX), and a SWIFT MT700 example (text) — and drop them in me/trade-docs/, then index that folder with docgraph (node lib/docgraph/indexer.js me/trade-docs). The live demo of docgraph searching/cross-referencing a real LC plus the vision-on-a-scan beat is the single thing 95% of applicants cannot show — and it is worthless if the documents aren't indexed before the call. Specimens only need to be representative; they're public templates. Treat this as a hard prerequisite for the Day-1 verify step and the interview demo.

memories.title: We'll use Dapper for reads, EF Core for writes
memories.content: Planned design: the write path will use EF Core via BGAPIDbContext (change tracking, migrations); the read path (GetFinancingRequestQuery) will use Dapper with QueryAsync and flat DTO projections (including the doc-reference fields). We assume Mitigram favors explicit SQL over ORMs — their JD says 'beyond ORMs' and lists Dapper — so a Dapper read side both matches how we think they work and lets us reframe the >30s to <3s SQL-optimization story through Dapper.

memories.title: We'll register handlers scoped, contrasting BGAPI singletons
memories.content: Planned: TradeFinance command/query handlers (and the ITradeDocumentExtractor) will be registered scoped (per request), sitting beside BGAPI's existing singleton service managers. We'll keep both deliberately so we can talk about DI lifetimes and the captive-dependency pitfall (a singleton holding a scoped dependency). We assume Mitigram, on .NET 8 + Mediator, registers handlers per-request the same way.

memories.title: We'll keep the invariant in the aggregate, not the controller
memories.content: Planned: FinancingRequest will enforce its own invariant — it cannot be Approved for an amount above the counterparty's credit limit. The rule will be a method on the aggregate (Approve(creditLimit)), never in the controller or handler, because we treat the aggregate as the consistency and transaction boundary. This is the DDD pattern we assume their domain code follows.

memories.title: We'll model Money, Iban and DocumentReference as immutable record value objects
memories.content: Planned: Money(decimal Amount, string Currency), Iban(string Value) and DocumentReference(string DocId, string Hash) will be C# records — immutable, equality-by-value, self-validating in their constructors (reject negative amounts, non-ISO currency, malformed IBAN). DocumentReference ties a request to the document it was extracted from (the audit/traceability story Mitigram cares about). The intent is to avoid primitive obsession and make invalid states unrepresentable, which is how we think a trade-finance domain should be modelled.

memories.title: We'll do CQRS via MediatR with validation + logging behaviors
memories.content: Planned: commands (CreateFinancingRequest carrying the extracted fields + DocumentReference, ApproveFinancingRequest) and a query (GetFinancingRequest) routed through MediatR, with a FluentValidation ValidationBehavior and a LoggingBehavior wrapping every handler. We assume Mitigram uses a mediator pipeline this way — but their JD says 'Mediator' generically, and MediatR's licensing change pushed some teams to the source-generator 'Mediator' alternative, so we should be ready it may not be MediatR specifically.

memories.title: We'll build it test-first with xUnit + Testcontainers
memories.content: Planned approach: build the credit-limit invariant, the missing-field gap-to-confirm behavior, and the command handlers red-green-refactor in xUnit (Moq for the extractor and the I/O boundary, FluentAssertions). Add one Testcontainers integration test that spins a real SQL Server in Docker and exercises Intake -> Approve -> Get. The tests will be the executable spec the AI agent works against and our safety net for reviewing generated code. We assume Mitigram's TDD culture expects this.

memories.title: We'll add a Redis cache-aside on read + JWT denylist
memories.content: Planned: a cache-aside (with TTL) on the GetFinancingRequest read path, and a Redis denylist for JWT revocation — since JWTs are stateless, revocation is the hard part. BGAPI already has Redis and JWT bearer (IdentityServer4 to Keycloak) wired, so we'll extend what's there rather than add new infra.

memories.title: We'll build one Svelte 4 component + a Vitest test
memories.content: Planned: one Svelte 4 component (FinancingRequestCard) consuming GET /api/financing-requests/{id} and rendering the EXTRACTED fields from a real document (plus any gaps) — a far stronger demo than a hand-typed DTO — with a Vitest test. The point is a small but real fullstack proof and a talking point — Svelte compiles to vanilla JS, no virtual DOM, reactivity via assignment ($:), which we'll contrast with Angular change detection. Angular stays our spoken strength (RxJS, DI, large-app structure).

memories.title: We'll deploy BGAPI to the k3s homelab
memories.content: Planned: containerize BGAPI (multi-stage Dockerfile) and deploy to the k3s cluster — Raspberry Pi 5 as master, Asus X540S as worker — with deployment/service/ingress/configmap+secret manifests, using nodeSelector to keep heavy pods off the 4GB Asus. The intent is hands-on Kubernetes proof, framed as 'conversant and hands-on at homelab scale', related to the Azure Service Fabric orchestration we've actually done.

memories.title: We'll keep the slice on net10.0; .NET 8 is the baseline
memories.content: Planned: keep the slice on net10.0 (BGAPI's framework) rather than refactoring the target, and be ready to say the C# 13 features Mitigram cares about (primary constructors, collection expressions, records, pattern matching, required members) all exist on .NET 8 — .NET 10 is a superset and nothing we build will depend on a .NET 10-only API. We assume they won't object as long as we can speak to the .NET 8 baseline.

memories.title: SQL optimization proof point: >30s to <3s
memories.content: Headline performance story: cut a critical search from >30s (frontend timeout) to <3s across 700K+ folders and 1M+ records. Replaced filesystem traversal plus blob scanning with an indexed SQL table plus covering indexes; integrated Azure Blob only for retrieval after the indexed lookup. The why: covering indexes (INCLUDE columns) avoid key lookups so the query never touches the base table.

memories.title: Aperio — my MCP-native AI memory layer (and document engine)
memories.content: I created and maintain Aperio: an open-source, self-hosted, MCP-native AI memory layer — 'one brain, every agent, nothing forgotten'. Local-first: local embeddings via Ollama, vectors in SQLite or PostgreSQL/pgvector, no cloud dependency — relevant for a fintech with data-residency concerns. Subsystems exposed as MCP tools: memory (remember/recall), wiki, codegraph (symbol index over repos), DOCGRAPH (document-interpretation engine over PDF/DOCX/XLSX/PPTX/EML/HTML — search/outline/context/refs), a VISION path (read_image/preprocess_image/describe_image via a configurable VLM), GitHub triage, and a generic SQL client. The piece most relevant to Mitigram is docgraph + vision — see the dedicated docgraph memory.

memories.title: Aperio docgraph — a document-interpretation engine (sibling of Mitigram's product)
memories.content: docgraph is Aperio's document subsystem and the strongest, most company-specific parallel to Mitigram. It is a pre-indexed graph over the user's document folders with a per-format extractor for Markdown, txt/rst, HTML, PDF, Word (.docx), Excel (.xlsx), PowerPoint (.pptx) and email (.eml); it chunks and embeds the content with local models, runs hybrid (vector + lexical) search, and extracts exact references — IDs, URLs, emails, citations — so doc_refs can find every document that mentions a given reference across a corpus. Exposed as five MCP tools: doc_repos, doc_search, doc_outline, doc_context, doc_refs. Same shape as Mitigram's pipeline: ingest -> normalize -> structure -> make-queryable -> cross-reference. For trade finance this is 'which documents reference LC number X / this counterparty / this invoice id', and (with the vision PoC) pixels -> fields on a scan. We did NOT build production OCR or a trained ML field-classifier — see the honest-bridge memory.

memories.title: Aperio — the idea: a privacy-first local co-pilot, not an autopilot
memories.content: Aperio's philosophy (real, ships today): it is a CO-PILOT, not an autopilot — it drafts and recalls, the human owns correctness and reviews every change. PRIVACY/local-first: everything lives on the user's machine, nothing syncs to a cloud by default; zero-config, one file (SQLite) holds memories, wiki and graphs; Postgres/pgvector optional for scale. SAFETY: write paths are confirm-before-write (e.g. db_execute and GitHub write tools preview the change and require the user's OK), reads run freely, untrusted input (issue text, documents) is treated as data never as instructions. This is the same posture a regulated fintech wants — local data residency, auditability, human-in-the-loop. Project lives at github.com/BaiGanio/aperio; there is also an Aperio-lite for non-code users.

memories.title: Aperio — the tech stack
memories.content: Aperio's stack (real): Node.js core; Model Context Protocol (MCP) as the agent interface (stdio server, ~50 tools); Ollama for local LLMs and embeddings; storage in SQLite by default or PostgreSQL + pgvector for vector search at scale; tree-sitter for codegraph parsing (C#, JS/TS, etc.); per-format document extractors for docgraph; a vision/VLM bridge (lib/helpers/imageBridge.js) routing images to a configurable model via OLLAMA_VLM_MODEL (code default qwen2.5vl:7b; this machine runs qwen3-vl:8b). Pluggable AI providers (local Ollama plus cloud options). Self-knowledge for the agent lives in id/capabilities.md (wired into the system prompt). It runs as a local web app/terminal and as an MCP server other agents (e.g. Claude Code) spawn over stdio.

memories.title: Octopus Energy / 4hundred proof point
memories.content: At Obecto I delivered 4hundred (Octopus Energy Germany), Germany's first fully digital energy retailer, building Azure Service Fabric microservices and consumer-facing Angular features that let 50K+ customers manage energy and switch providers via mobile by 2022.

memories.title: Planned demo: Aperio indexes BGAPI + real trade docs for live AI-onboarding
memories.content: We plan a two-beat live demo. Beat 1 (AI-onboarding): Aperio's codegraph indexes BGAPI's C# (.cs via tree-sitter), so in a Claude Code session opened in BGAPI the agent answers architecture questions (code_search, code_callers) and recalls stored decisions (recall) live — the 'agents that understand our architecture' workflow Mitigram's ad describes. Beat 2 (document interpretation): docgraph indexes real sample trade documents (LC PDF, invoice DOCX, SWIFT MT700) so doc_search/doc_refs search and cross-reference trade content, and the vision PoC pulls fields off a scan. The Aperio capabilities are real today; the rehearsed demo and the indexed sample docs are what we still need to set up (see the trade-docs ACTION memory).

memories.title: Trade finance domain basics + document vocabulary
memories.content: Trade finance funds international trade and mitigates risk between importer and exporter (payment vs delivery). Instruments: Letters of Credit (LC, import/export), bank guarantees and standby LCs, invoice/receivables financing. Document vocabulary to sound native: the STRUCTURED side is SWIFT MT — MT700 = LC issuance, MT707 = amendment, MT760 = guarantee/SBLC, MT799 = free-format; the UNSTRUCTURED side is PDFs/scans/emails. UCP 600 governs documentary credits, and discrepancy checking — do the presented documents match the LC terms? — is the core operational pain (a discrepancy delays payment). The goal is straight-through processing (STP): minimal manual touch. Discrepancy checking and 'populate missing data' are extract-fields-and-compare problems — exactly the document-to-data engine I built. My regulated-domain background (military/aerospace, energy, legal) transfers directly: correctness, audit trails, compliance.

memories.title: The document-driven flow we'll build and whiteboard
memories.content: The flow we'll build and be ready to whiteboard: a trade document is uploaded to the intake endpoint, ITradeDocumentExtractor (text or vision impl) returns fields + gaps, the controller sends CreateFinancingRequestCommand via MediatR, then ValidationBehavior (FluentValidation), then LoggingBehavior, then the handler creates the FinancingRequest aggregate (with a DocumentReference), the aggregate enforces its credit-limit invariant on Approve, the repository saves via EF, and the read side returns a flat DTO via Dapper. In one line: document -> extractor -> command -> behavior -> handler -> aggregate (+DocumentReference) -> repo -> Dapper read.

memories.title: Diagnosing a slow SQL query
memories.content: Approach: read the execution plan (scans vs seeks, key lookups, spools), SET STATISTICS IO/TIME ON, treat missing-index hints with skepticism, watch for parameter sniffing, check DMV waits. Fix via indexing strategy (covering indexes), query rewrite, and removing N+1. Clustered index = table physically ordered by key (one per table); non-clustered = a separate structure with pointers.

memories.title: JWT validation and revocation pattern
memories.content: Validate JWTs on signature, issuer, audience, and expiry; use short-lived access tokens plus refresh tokens. Revocation is the hard part for stateless tokens — keep a denylist in Redis. SSO/MFA flow to know: OAuth2 authorization-code + PKCE; OIDC is the identity layer on top of OAuth2 (id_token); SAML is the older XML-based enterprise SSO.

memories.title: Gap-honesty framing for the interview
memories.content: For real gaps (DDD/CQRS vocabulary, Dapper, TDD depth, hands-on K8s) we won't bluff. Once the slice is built, the framing is: state where I am, then show the evidence — 'I built a document-driven DDD/CQRS slice with a Dapper read side, test-first, and deployed it to my own k3s cluster — want to see the diff?' For document AI specifically, use the OCR/ML bridge (separate memory): built the engine + a vision PoC, not their production model. Until the slice exists, lead with the plan, not the claim. Senior interviewers respect 'here's exactly where I am plus what I'm building' over hand-waving.

memories.title: The proof points connect into one document-led sentence
memories.content: The pitch we're building toward, to be true by interview day: 'Mitigram turns unstructured trade documents into structured data with OCR + ML. I've already shipped a document-ingestion engine — docgraph, with a vision front-end — inside my own AI platform, and I rebuilt a slice of your exact flow on my .NET codebase: a trade document goes in, structured data and a financing decision come out.' It fuses Aperio/docgraph (the document-interpretation parallel + AI tooling), the SQL/Dapper strength, and the Azure microservices background with the new document-driven build. The Aperio/docgraph/vision threads are already real; the fourth — the build — is what this plan delivers.

memories.title: Model choices for the live demo (agent vs vision are separate)
memories.content: Models for the live demo, two SEPARATE slots — do not conflate them. AGENT/tool-calling (driving Aperio's tools, recall, codegraph/docgraph): run gemma4:12b — per dev-side.md it uses every Aperio tool reliably, chains them without losing the thread, and reasons well; keep gemma4:e4b as a lightweight fallback; avoid the tiny qwen2.5:3b for the demo. VISION/VLM (the OCR step, image->fields): DECIDED = qwen3-vl:8b, set via OLLAMA_VLM_MODEL=qwen3-vl:8b (Aperio's code default is qwen2.5vl:7b — override it). qwen3-vl:8b leads OCRBench and fits the 16 GB machine at ~11 GB Q4_K_M. gemma4 is the agent model, qwen3-vl:8b is the vision model — never swap them.

memories.title: Where the Mitigram prep materials live
memories.content: My Mitigram prep lives in the aperio repo me/ folder. Read mitigram-product-and-docgraph.md FIRST (their product + the docgraph/vision blend + the honest OCR/ML bridge + SWIFT/UCP vocabulary). Then: mitigram-master-plan.md (the 10-day document-driven build plan), mitigram-interview-qa.md (Q&A for both rounds, incl. section B.0 on their product), capstone-takehome-sim.md (the document-shaped simulated take-home plus rubric), aperio-bgapi-integration.md (wiring Aperio into BGAPI + indexing sample trade docs + the vision PoC), PROGRESS.md (the build tracker), plan-me.md (the BGAPI audit), dev-side.md (local model roster; vision model DECIDED = qwen3-vl:8b), and me/trade-docs/ (the sample trade documents to source — see the ACTION memory).

memories.title: C# 13 features used in the slice
memories.content: Latest-language-feature talking points, each used in the slice: primary constructors, collection expressions ([1,2,3]), records with init-only setters (the value objects — Money, Iban, DocumentReference), pattern matching (switch expressions, property and list patterns), required members, and nullable reference types. The job ad explicitly asks for 'latest language features.'

wiki_articles.title: Mitigram Take-Home — Document-Driven TradeFinance Slice (PLAN / Work in Progress)
wiki_articles.summary: PLANNED walkthrough of the document-driven TradeFinance slice we intend to build for Mitigram interview prep: ingest a trade document -> extract fields -> FinancingRequest aggregate -> approve -> Dapper read, mirroring Mitigram's product (turning trade documents into structured data). DDD, CQRS/Mediator, EF write + Dapper read, TDD, Svelte/Vitest, k3s — all AI-assisted via Aperio (docgraph + vision). Work in progress, not yet built.
wiki_articles.body_md:
# Mitigram Take-Home — Document-Driven TradeFinance Slice (PLAN / Work in Progress)

> **Status: planned, not built yet.** This is the design and intent for a take-home-style slice we'll build over ~10 days of interview prep. Read it as 'what we'll do and why', not as finished work.

**Why this shape.** Mitigram's product turns unstructured trade documents (and structured SWIFT messages) into structured, actionable trade-finance data using OCR + ML. So the slice is deliberately *document-driven*: a trade document goes in, structured data and a financing decision come out — mirroring their flow rather than synthetic CRUD.

**The flow.** `POST /api/financing-requests/intake` (multipart: an invoice/LC as PDF/DOCX, or an image for the vision PoC) -> an extraction step yields fields -> `CreateFinancingRequestCommand` carries them -> `FinancingRequest` aggregate (Draft->Submitted) -> `ApproveFinancingRequestCommand` (credit-limit invariant) -> `GetFinancingRequestQuery` (Dapper read).

**Document intake + extraction (the part that mirrors their product).** Extraction sits behind `ITradeDocumentExtractor`, returning fields PLUS a list of gaps. Two impls: a deterministic text/DOCX parser, and a vision impl (hand a scan to Aperio's `read_image`/`describe_image` path; the local VLM is `qwen3-vl:8b`, set via `OLLAMA_VLM_MODEL` — it leads OCRBench and fits the 16 GB machine at ~11 GB Q4_K_M). A field the document lacks becomes a **gap to confirm**, not a crash — the 'populate missing data' nod. **Honesty boundary: we built the ingestion engine and INTEGRATED a strong open VLM as the OCR step — we did not train it and did not build Mitigram's trained ML field-classification pipeline; the seam is where their model drops in.**

**Domain (DDD).** `FinancingRequest` is the aggregate root and transaction boundary. `Money`, `Iban` and `DocumentReference` are immutable record value objects that validate in their constructors, so invalid states are unrepresentable. The one business invariant — a request cannot be approved above the counterparty credit limit — lives inside the aggregate (`Approve(creditLimit)`), never in the controller. `DocumentReference` ties the request to its source document for audit.

**Application (CQRS + Mediator).** Writes and reads are split. The commands go through MediatR to scoped handlers, wrapped by a FluentValidation behavior and a logging behavior; `GetFinancingRequestQuery` is the read side. Handlers are scoped — a deliberate contrast with BGAPI's singleton service managers.

**Data (EF + Dapper).** EF Core owns the write side (change tracking, migrations). The read side uses Dapper — explicit SQL into a flat DTO (incl. the doc-reference fields) — with a Redis cache-aside. This is where my SQL background shows (I once cut a search from >30s to <3s across 1M+ records with covering indexes).

**Tests (TDD).** The invariant, the missing-field gap behavior, and the handlers are built red-green-refactor in xUnit; a Testcontainers integration test spins a real SQL Server in Docker and runs Intake -> Approve -> Get. The tests double as the executable spec the AI agent works against.

**Frontend.** One Svelte 4 component (with a Vitest test) renders the **extracted** fields from a real document — a far stronger demo than a hand-typed DTO.

**Deployment.** Containerize and deploy to my own k3s homelab (Raspberry Pi master + Asus worker) with deployment/service/ingress/configmap+secret manifests.

**The AI angle (my edge).** The whole build runs inside Claude Code with Aperio — my open-source, MCP-native memory layer — whose codegraph indexes BGAPI's C# and whose **docgraph indexes real sample trade documents** so the agent can search/cross-reference them, plus a vision path for scans. The AI drafts; I review every diff like a junior's PR and own correctness.

**One sentence (target, true by interview day).** Mitigram turns unstructured trade documents into structured data with OCR + ML; I've shipped a document-ingestion engine (docgraph + vision) and rebuilt a slice of their exact flow on my .NET codebase — document in, structured data and a financing decision out.

wiki_articles.title: Aperio — What It Is (self-knowledge)
wiki_articles.summary: Self-knowledge article: Aperio is an open-source, self-hosted, MCP-native AI memory layer and document engine. Local-first/privacy by default, co-pilot not autopilot, confirm-before-write safety. Subsystems: memory, wiki, codegraph, docgraph (document interpretation), a vision/VLM path, GitHub triage, and a SQL client. The docgraph + vision pieces are the sibling of Mitigram's document-interpretation product.
wiki_articles.body_md:
# Aperio — What It Is

**Tagline.** *One brain. Every agent. Nothing forgotten.* Aperio is an open-source, self-hosted, **MCP-native** personal memory layer **and document engine** for AI agents (github.com/BaiGanio/aperio). There is also an **Aperio-lite** for non-code users.

**The idea.**
- **Co-pilot, not autopilot.** It drafts, recalls, and connects context; the human owns correctness and reviews every change.
- **Privacy / local-first.** Everything lives on the user's machine; nothing syncs to a cloud by default. Zero-config — one SQLite file holds memories, wiki and graphs. Postgres + pgvector optional for scale.
- **Safety.** Write paths are confirm-before-write (e.g. `db_execute`, GitHub write tools preview and require the user's OK); reads run freely; untrusted input (documents, issue text) is data, never instructions. This posture maps directly onto what a regulated fintech wants: data residency, auditability, human-in-the-loop.

**The stack.** Node.js core; **Model Context Protocol (MCP)** as the agent interface (stdio server, ~50 tools); **Ollama** for local LLMs + embeddings; **SQLite** by default or **PostgreSQL + pgvector** at scale; **tree-sitter** for code parsing; per-format extractors for documents; a **vision/VLM bridge** (`lib/helpers/imageBridge.js`) routing images to a configurable model via `OLLAMA_VLM_MODEL` (code default `qwen2.5vl:7b`; this machine runs `qwen3-vl:8b`). Pluggable AI providers (local Ollama + cloud options). The agent's own self-knowledge lives in `id/capabilities.md`.

**Subsystems (exposed as MCP tools).**
- **Memory** — typed, tagged notes (`remember`/`recall`/`update_memory`/`forget`); the source of truth.
- **Wiki** — LLM-authored articles summarizing clusters of memories; a derived view.
- **Codegraph** — symbol index over indexed repos (`code_search`/`code_callers`/…).
- **Docgraph** — a **document-interpretation engine**: per-format extractors (PDF/DOCX/XLSX/PPTX/EML/HTML/MD/txt) -> chunk + embed -> hybrid search -> reference extraction; five tools `doc_repos`/`doc_search`/`doc_outline`/`doc_context`/`doc_refs`.
- **Vision** — `read_image`/`preprocess_image`/`describe_image`: pixels -> text/fields via a local VLM.
- **GitHub** — issue read + triage; **Databases** — a generic, parameterized SQL client over the user's own DBs.

**Why it matters for Mitigram.** docgraph + the vision path are the already-built **sibling** of Mitigram's product (AI document interpretation for trade finance): the same arc of ingest -> normalize -> structure -> make-queryable -> cross-reference, plus a pixels->fields PoC. We did **not** build production OCR or a trained ML field-classifier — that is Mitigram's IP; we built the engine around it and designed the seam it slots into.
